cataloging_subject.label: The Chronicle, Part II
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The complete second part of Robert Mannyng's Middle English verse Chronicle (also called the Story of Inglande), from Cadwaladr through the death of Edward I, as selected and first printed by Thomas Hearne. This record does not describe Mannyng's Part I, Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle as a separate work, the whole 1725 or 1810 composite publication, Hearne's appended antiquarian tracts, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Chronicle, Part II
catalog_summary.author: Robert Mannyng of Brunne
catalog_summary.date: Composed during the reign of Edward III and completed 15 May 1338
catalog_summary.region: Completed at Sixhills, Lincolnshire, England; full composition geography not established

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number for source copy
Inner Temple shelfmark for base witness

notes: The modern preferred title identifies the edited selection. Folger catalogs Sullens's complete-work edition as The Chronicle; 'Part II' prevents this record from being mistaken for the whole two-part work.

titles.value: The Story of Inglande, Part II
Peter Langtoft's Chronicle (as illustrated and improv'd by Robert of Brunne), from the Death of Cadwalader to the End of King Edward the First's Reign
notes: Hearne's publication title foregrounds Langtoft, but the printed Middle English text is Mannyng's translation and adaptation.

agents.name: Robert Mannyng of Brunne
notes: Middle English author, translator, and adapter; the cataloged Part II is chiefly based on Pierre de Langtoft but includes additional material and Mannyng's own shaping.

agents.name: Pierre de Langtoft
notes: Author of the Anglo-Norman source chronicle; not the author of Mannyng's Middle English wording.

agents.name: Thomas Hearne
notes: Editor and transcriber of the 1725 first edition, reprinted in 1810.
Authorship here names the creator of the Middle English work, not the author of its principal Anglo-Norman source.

scope.description: Chiefly translates the second part of Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle

notes: Mannyng adds material from other written and oral sources and expands or reshapes Langtoft; 'translation' alone would understate the work.

scope.description: Hearne's 1725 edited selection of Part II, retained in the 1810 reset reprint
Transcription of Mannyng's Part II from Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7
Anglo-Norman specimens and comparison notes from three French Langtoft manuscripts

notes: The French manuscripts supplied comparison material in the footnotes; they were not base witnesses for Mannyng's Middle English text.

scope.description: Preface and embedded historical, textual, and Anglo-Norman notes surrounding the edited poem

date_statements.display: During the reign of Edward III, completed on the Ides of May (15 May) 1338
notes: The Petyt colophon has inconsistent dominical-letter and regnal-year details, but scholarship identifies the intended year as 1338, when the Ides of May fell on Friday. The year range records Edward III's reign, not a claim that all of Part II was continuously composed from 1327.

date_statements.display: Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7, second half of the fourteenth century to around 1400; possibly about 1375
notes: This is a witness date, not the composition date. The MED bibliography uses about 1400, while recent linguistic scholarship cites a second-half-fourteenth-century date, possibly about 1375.

Mannyng's autobiographical prologue associates his writing of the Story with the Gilbertine house at Sixhills, and scholarship says he finished the chronicle there. This does not prove that every stage of composition occurred at Sixhills. London and Oxford are edition or repository places, not medieval composition places.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English commentary, Latin formulas, and Anglo-Norman comparison passages belong to Hearne's edition and apparatus and do not change the cataloged work's primary language.

form_statements.description: A continuous Middle English rhymed verse chronicle; Hearne's prose and multilingual footnotes are edition-level apparatus.

subject_statements.label: Reign of Edward I and Anglo-Scottish conflict

scope.description: The chronicle's extended concluding historical sequence

summaries.value: Part II of Robert Mannyng's Chronicle recounts English history from the transition after Cadwaladr through the death and burial of Edward I. It follows Pierre de Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle closely while adding, expanding, and reshaping material from other written and oral traditions. Mannyng presents the past through sequences of rulers, wars, political counsel, moral judgment, and contested succession, ending with his authorial leave-taking and a dated colophon.

Hearne deliberately omitted Mannyng's Part I and first published only Part II. The U-M encoding narrows the composite book further: its searchable text retains Part II on printed pages 1-341 and Hearne's embedded notes, while most of the publisher's preface, glossary, index, Glastonbury roll, Scrooby account, Roman-antiquities tract, and Stonehenge tract survive only as omitted-matter page milestones or linked page images.

extent.description: One continuous verse work. The XML contains 8,365 L elements overall: 8,357 direct lines in the main poem plus eight L elements nested inside Hearne's footnotes. Modern reference descriptions commonly report 8,358 lines for Part II, so the value here is explicitly an encoding-specific metric rather than a universal critical line count.

content_parts.label: Part II: Cadwaladr to the death of Edward I
content_parts.summary: Mannyng's Middle English continuation of English history, chiefly translating and adapting the second part of Langtoft's Anglo-Norman Chronicle.
notes: The XML has no reliable intrinsic chapter hierarchy. Its 304 LG containers are representation units, not asserted authorial chapters.

related_works.title: The Chronicle (The Story of Inglande)
notes: The cataloged selection is the complete second of the whole Chronicle's two parts; Hearne deliberately omitted Part I.

related_works.title: The Chronicle of Pierre de Langtoft
notes: Part II chiefly translates Langtoft's corresponding Anglo-Norman history but is not a mechanically exact translation and does not depend on any one identified surviving French manuscript.

evidence.locator: HEADER; TITLEPAGE; BODY/DIV1, printed pp. 1-341; final authorial leave-taking and colophon
evidence.summary: Supplies the immediate 1810 source statement, U-M identifier and production note, Hearne title, complete encoded Part II, opening at Cadwaladr, conclusion at Edward I's death and burial, authorial ending, embedded apparatus, and omitted composite matter.

evidence.citation: Element, hierarchy, and milestone inspection of repository aba2096.xml.
evidence.locator: TEXT/FRONT, BODY, and BACK; DIV1, LG, L, P, NOTE1, and PB elements
evidence.summary: Counts 946 PB milestones, 304 LG elements, 8,365 L elements overall, 8,357 direct poem lines, and eight lines nested in Hearne notes. The searchable body covers printed pp. 1-341; 229 front and 377 back page milestones represent chiefly omitted composite matter.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item ABA2096.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Confirms current public HTML access, the 1810 publication source, stable identifier, page-view links, and U-M's current public-domain and No Copyright-United States statements.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, catalog record 93020, The Chronicle, Robert Mannyng of Brunne, ed. Idelle Sullens (1996).
evidence.summary: Catalogs Mannyng as author and the text as Middle English verse; states that Part I chiefly translates Wace and Part II derives from Peter of Langtoft.

evidence.citation: Jacqueline M. Burek, 'Of diuers kynd: Robert Mannyng's Story of Inglande,' in Literary Variety and the Writing of History in Britain's Long Twelfth Century (Boydell & Brewer, 2023), pp. 186-221.
evidence.summary: Identifies Story of Inglande as also known as the Chronicle, describes Mannyng as a Gilbertine canon, and treats the work as a primarily faithful but independently shaped translation of Wace and Langtoft drawing on additional written and oral sources.

evidence.citation: Ruth Crosby, 'Robert Mannyng of Brunne: A New Biography,' PMLA 57, no. 1 (1942): 15-28.
evidence.summary: Prints and interprets the Petyt colophon: despite inconsistent dominical-letter and regnal-year details, the Ides of May fell on Friday in 1338, establishing the intended completion year and date.

evidence.citation: Matthew Fisher, Once Called Albion: The Composition and Transmission of History Writing in England, 1280-1350 (University of Oxford DPhil thesis, 2005).
evidence.summary: Treats Mannyng and Langtoft as sophisticated derivative historiography and cites the conclusion of Mannyng's Part II as lines 8357-8358, supporting the modern 8,358-line lineation against which the XML metric is compared.

evidence.citation: Ryan Perry, 'Robert Mannyng and the Imagined Reading Communities for Handlyng Synne,' in Pastoral Care in Medieval England: Interdisciplinary Approaches (Routledge, 2019).
evidence.summary: States that Mannyng reports finishing his verse chronicle in 1338 at Sixhills Priory, a Gilbertine house in Lincolnshire.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, Middle English Bibliography entry BIB2350, The Chronicle of England, Part 1.
evidence.summary: Identifies London, Inner Temple Library, Petyt 511, vol. 7; dates its Mannyng text about 1400 after a 1338 original; records one hand and Lincolnshire localization; and identifies Hearne 1725, reprinted 1810, as an edition from Petyt 511.

evidence.citation: Sara M. Pons-Sanz and Sean Roberts, 'The Integration of Norse-Derived Terms in English: Effects of Formal Similarity,' Transactions of the Philological Society (2025).
evidence.summary: Uses Petyt 511.7 for Mannyng's Chronicle, calls it the only localized Lincolnshire manuscript of Mannyng's texts, and dates it to the second half of the fourteenth century, possibly about 1375, citing Sullens and eLALME LP 38.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for aba2096, Hearne edition research.
evidence.summary: Binds title, selection boundary, editorial roles, composite contents, and embedded apparatus to direct inspection of the 1810 source volumes and Hearne's preface.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for aba2096, Petyt manuscript research.
evidence.summary: Binds the base-witness identification, date range, material, hand, dialect, and public-surrogate limits to the Middle English Compendium, Hearne, and Inner Temple evidence.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for aba2096, Langtoft source-work and manuscript research.
evidence.summary: Binds the Anglo-Norman source-work relation and current identification of the College of Arms manuscript to Wright's manuscript census and edition.

open_questions.question: How should the published datings of Inner Temple Library, Petyt MS 511, vol. 7—possibly about 1375 and around 1400—be reconciled?
next_steps: Consult J. C. Davies's 1972 Inner Temple manuscript catalogue entry, Sullens's codicological discussion, and current eLALME profile 38 side by side.
Keep the witness date separate from Mannyng's 1338 composition date and do not prefer the narrowest date without direct palaeographical evidence.

open_questions.question: Why does the XML contain 8,357 direct poem lines when modern descriptions commonly give 8,358 lines for Part II?
next_steps: Compare the final lineation in Petyt 511, Hearne 1725/1810, and Sullens 1996.
Retain the explicit XML metric until a specific omitted, joined, or differently counted line is identified.

notes: The XML header inherits a generic catalog title and AUTHOR entry for Peter of Langtoft. Direct inspection shows that the encoded Middle English body is Robert Mannyng's Part II; Langtoft is the source author, not the author of this English wording.
The XML's subject keywords describe the whole composite 1725/1810 publication. Glastonbury Abbey, Scrooby, Roman antiquities, and Stonehenge are not subjects of the cataloged Part II.
No date, dialect, holding, access condition, or image-rights statement belonging to Petyt 511 or the three French collation witnesses is assigned wholesale to the abstract work.
